Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-CD09F9D8 Deadline (Brussels Time) 01 Oct 2024 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
We're taking on this initiative in youth work because it's critical to address the ways in which media influences the attitudes,
behaviors, and overall condition of young people. The issue is that different media platforms, including social media and
conventional media, have a significant impact on how young people perceive the world, form opinions, and experience
mental health issues.
This initiative has a variety of objectives. First and foremost, we wish to educate youth about the potential benefits and
drawbacks of social media. We want youngsters to be selective about the information they consume, aware of biases, and
able to select healthy media. Additionally, we wish to support youth in developing their media literacy. We aim to teach
young people how to use the internet sensibly and show them how media can support their development and contribute to
constructive social change. Our project also includes a significant component on promoting positive media messages. We
want to develop campaigns that address significant issues, involve all people, and inspire youth to contribute to improving
social media for their communities and for themselves. We're also investigating the potential impact of social media on
mental health. Our goal is to create resources and programs that assist youth in managing the potential harm that media
may do to their emotional state.
In conclusion, our work with young people involves identifying and addressing the various ways that media has an impact on
young people. We aim to support people in spreading positive messages, being mindful, better understanding media, and
taking care of their mental health in the digital age.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The project’s main activity is the youth exchange that will happen in 2025 June in Balatonkeresztúr, Hungary. We have
designed a comprehensive set of activities for the project.
Initially, participants will be introduced to the Youth Exchange and Erasmus+, followed by team-building games to foster a
positive group dynamic. After this, the main focus will be on social media, encompassing an examination of trends in each
partner country, a structured debate on the merits and demerits of social media, and a role-playing exercise exploring
authenticity online. Small groups will also engage in discussions on current social media trends. Continuing to the theme of
building healthy relationships online, seminars and workshops will be conducted to explore the impact of social media on
various relationships, provide practical tips for positive online communication, and facilitate the creation of digital vision
boards and a toolkit for digital selfcare.
A pivotal section of the project will dig into understanding the impact of violent media on youth behaviour. This will involve in-
depth discussions, a media literacy game focusing on distinguishing factual and fictional information, structured debates on
the influence of violent media, and a teamwork activity combating misinformation by creating a digital board. Midway
through the project, there will be a free day with a city quest in Keszthely. Then , there will be an emphasis on digital safety
will be realized through a SafeClick seminar, a media influence trivia game, a case study on the application of SafeClick,
and a cyber sleuth challenge. The last day of the project will involve an evaluation and feedback session, allowing
participants to provide insights into the project's performance, training sessions, and discussions.
As for the participants, a total number of 35 participants will be involved, 6 in every team + 1 team leader. They will come
from 5 different countries from 5 different European regions. We will have a diverse group of young individuals, within an
age range of 18-25, from various socio-economic, linguistic, educational backgrounds. This will include participants with
fewer opportunities in every team, namely participants with economical and geographical barriers. The participants will be
selected by the partner organizations, in a democratic process, based on their motivation and interests. Participants with
fewer opportunities will have an advantage in the application process.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
We aim for our efforts to bring about several positive effects. One key goal is to help young people develop a deeper
understanding of the various ways media impacts their lives. By raising awareness, we hope to empower them to make
informed choices about the media they engage with.
We also aim to improve media literacy. Our hope is that young people will gain the skills necessary to navigate the digital

world effectively, learning to distinguish between reliable sources of information and those that may be biased. This, in turn,
should help them become more discerning media consumers.
Another important aspect of our project is to encourage positive social contributions. We want to motivate young people to
participate actively in shaping a more positive narrative on social media, promoting inclusivity and positive messaging. We
believe this could foster a greater sense of community and shared responsibility among them.
In addition, we are focused on supporting mental well-being. By exploring the connection between media exposure and
mental health, we hope to develop programs that help young people manage any negative impacts media might have on
their mental health, and encourage healthier, more constructive interactions with media content.
Furthermore, we want to empower young people to use media as a tool for personal growth and social change. Our goal is
to inspire them to actively shape a media environment that reflects their values and supports their personal development.
Ultimately, our project is intended to contribute to a long-term cultural shift of the participants. We aim to cultivate a
generation of young people who engage with media thoughtfully, becoming active contributors to a media landscape that
benefits individuals, rather than simply passive consumers.
While this initiative may represent only a small step, we believe it has the potential to create a ripple effect. Young people
can play a crucial role in spreading these ideas and driving positive changes within their communities, helping to build a
more welcoming and positive online space.
In addition to these goals, we also expect the more general impacts and outcomes of the Erasmus+ youth exchange
programs, which greatly contribute to both personal and professional development. These include cultural awareness,
fostering intercultural communication and dialogue, enhancing participants' key competencies, as well as developing soft
skills through various non-formal learning methods.

KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-CD09F9D8 Deadline (Brussels Time) 01 Oct 2024 12:00:00
Please briefly present your organisation/the group.
The Street of Youth Association is a youth organization based in Budapest, Hungary, and composed of Generation Z
members, specifically focusing on the challenges, problems, and issues faced by Generation Z youth (18-25 years old
youngsters). Our specialty is that we achieve this primarily by focusing on international projects and non-formal education.
We believe in the power of non-formal education and in the power of Erasmus+. We understand that Gen Z young people
are greatly helped through these project, especially with starting their careers, finding their path, and developing their soft
skills.
Our members are exclusively Generation Z youth who want to contribute to their own community and support other young
people. In the association, both the management and the local programs, as well as the international projects, are carried
out by young people, for young people. We believe this is the most effective way to truly make an impact on the lives of other
young people, engage with them and have a positive influence on our environment.
We can identify with the fact that this generation has completely different needs and is driven by different topics compared
to previous generations.
Accordingly, there are three topics that we prioritize and consider the most important in our organization: the mental health
of Gen Z young people; environment protection and sustainability; and the challenges related to the digital world, particularly
social media.
We also believe in youth empowerment. It is important to mention that our association operates on a similar principle to a
startup: if any of our members have a good idea or a passion project, the Street of Youth Association provides the
opportunity and a supportive environment for them to realize it.
What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
Although we are a young association, established only in February 2023, our members, who are mostly university students
and young professionals, have significant experience as participants in Erasmus+ youth exchanges, training courses, and
youth participation projects. We are working to utilize our experience and knowledge to create high-quality non-formal
learning mobility opportunities.
Street of Youth Association is a newcomer organization in Erasmus+. However, we are currently working hard on projects in
the previously mentioned topics, and we want to tackle the challenges we have experienced with other young people and
make an impact on our environment and young peers through non-formal learning.
Regarding the mental health of Gen Z young people, we particularly focus on work-life balance, the challenges around
starting careers, and excessive online presence. For the latter, a podcast series is in the pipeline, led by young people and
featuring experts, which is currently in the planning phase.
Regarding environmental protection and sustainability, the topics of climate anxiety, sustainable habits, and climate activism
are of particular concern to our organization and members. We are currently preparing an Erasmus+ youth exchange aimed
at developing sustainable habits, which may take place in 2025.
Regarding the challenges arising from digitalization among young people, our focus is on the conscious use of social media,
mindful media consumption and critical thinking, as well as screen time management.
We place special emphasis on organizing as many local programs as possible, or our members and other interested young
people so that members can connect offline, such as hiking together, playing soccer, and board games.
As well as promoting Erasmus+, Visegrad, and other international short-term mobility programs, to provide young people
with broad opportunities to participate in these initiatives. We have noticed that young people have limited access to these
opportunities, and many are not even aware of them. We want to democratize this as much as possible and enable more
young people to explore Europe, connect with their European peers, and learn about topics that concern them through non-
formal opportunities. We pay special attention to social media in this regard.

experience that they will bring to the project.

- Active in nature with outdoor activities as well as hikes and trips
- Managing partnerships and ongoing projects at local and international level as well

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
The Municipality of Cento is a local authority situated in the province of Ferrara, within the Emilia-Romagna
Region. It has a strategic geographic position, being almost equidistant by three important cities of the area as Bologna,
Modena, and Ferrara, and its extension is characterized by the presence of nine populous hamlets far from the city center.
Cento has a population of 35,174 inhabitants, of which 11,3% are foreign nationals, the majority coming from Morocco,
Pakistan and Romania.
The most important economic sectors of the city are the agricultural and the industrial one which, together,
represent the backbone of the socioeconomic city’s fabric, counting excellent enterprises in the field of mechanic and
automotive engineering.
The Municipality of Cento is well known for its historical carnival, dating back to 1600, and for the painting of the local
Baroque painter called “Guercino”, capable to attract every year in the city thousands of visitors from all over the world.
Therefore, as a public body, it benefits from experience and versatile skills and can fully and competently undertake work in
the following: The municipality promotes a policy of active participation in local life and defends the values of the European
Union, including respect for human dignity, freedom, democracy, equality, the rule of law, respect for human rights, including
the rights of persons belonging to minorities.
Active participation, intercultural dialogue, and mutual understanding, as well as combating stereotypes, have been
promoted through local bodies, as the “Youth Council”. This entity of participatory democracy foster close dialogue with
policy makers and the youths are often involved in international learning exchanges, sharing good practices and developing
structured dialogue.
What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your
organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
In the last years, the Municipality of Cento has shown a strong interest in European projects, acquiring experience in project
writing and management, working side by side with national and international partners, thanks to the establishment and
stabilization of a specific and specialized department dedicated to the design, development and implementation of
European and national projects, to find and create a range of opportunities for the city and its citizens.

The Municipality of Cento has demonstrated to be a quality and reliable partner, working on projects funded by different
European programs such as Life, Erasmus+, Urbact, Europe forCitizens and CERV, developing a structured network of
partners and stakeholders composed of European municipalities, NGOs and private companies specialized in the projects
management.
Moreover, the Municipality of Cento results is extremely involved in the topics of youth mobility and youth professional
formation and has, within its organogram, specific offices and staff specialized in these fields.
Sure enough, within these specific thematics, the Municipality of Cento can take advantage of the skills and expertise
acquired thanks to the longtime work of “Informa Giovani”,an office composed by specialized staff able to provide different
services to the territory’s youths, as formal and non-formal education, training courses, job orientation, dissemination of
mobility opportunity and much more.
This office, thanks to the brilliant work done in the last years, has become a landmark not only for the Municipality of Cento,
but for the entire region, sincethat, more than 3000 young people coming from the provinces of Modena, Bologna and
Ferrara resort to its services for their personal and professional development. The deep involvement in the youth field is
simply demonstrable, not only by the series of national and international projects having the youths as undisputed main
characters, but also by the relevant connections that the Municipality of Cento was able to establish with renowned
organizations, universities and public authorities working on the same fields.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
Umberto Amoroso is the representative of the “European Policies Office” of the Municipality of Cento. He has a bachelor's
degree in “International and Diplomatic Sciences” at the University of Bologna and, successively, he has obtained two
Master degrees in “Eastern European Studies” (University of Bologna) and “European Project Management” at University
“La Sapienza” of Rome. Thanks to this strong academic knowledge, he started to actualize his studies working as NGOs
worker in the Balkan area for two years, gaining experience in the planning, management and evaluation of projects co-
funded by the European Union in the fields of local development and youth empowerment.
Currently, he manages the European projects of Municipality of Cento, dealing with the research and development of project
ideas and funding opportunities at local, national and European level, elaborating and coordinating grant proposals in
different programs as Erasmus+, Life, CERV, Europe for Citizens and EuropeAid and establishing collaboration,
cooperation and networking with other organizations. More in detail, he boasted more than 2 years of experience in writing,
management and implementation of projects co-financed by the European Union.
Erika Bergamini is the contact person and supervisor of the “Youth Information Office” of the Municipality of Cento. She
graduated in “Political and Social Sciences” at the University of Bologna and has more than 10 years of experience in
planning and management of regional, national and European projects regarding the youth policies, focusing on the
development of offers concerning European mobility, training and exchange of best practices for youths in the field of job
placement.
During her activity within the Municipality of Cento, the users’ percentage of “Youth Information Office” is increased of 40%,
allowing, in this way, to thousands of young people from the provinces of Ferrara, Modena and Bologna to benefit from the
services of the office. Moreover, on the behalf of the Municipality of Cento, she deals with the development of a network with
other Municipalities, NGOs and Universities qualified in the development of projects pertinent to youth matters. Clear
example of that, is the productive collaboration established with the University of Bologna.

Would you like to make any comments or add any information to the summary of your organisation’s past participation?
For the munincipality of Cento from Italy, the topic of the project results to be extremely interesting and for the users of the services provided by the Youth Department. As a matter of
fact, this department of the Municipality of Cento, every year, counts more than 1500 accesses from youths belonging not only from the Cento's Municipality, but also from the bordering
provinces of Bologna and Modena, in order to receive the following services:
- high school and university's orientation;
- job matching;
- languages training courses;
- working formative paths;
- research of working and formative opportunities abroad;
- creation of CV;
- training for university's and working interviews.
All these services are organized and managed by professional youth workers working for the Municipality of Cento, which, during the last years and especially after the COVID
pandemic crisis, have noticed how phenomena of anxiety, depression, inadequacy and deep fear for the future are becoming even more popular and recurring among youths.
Therefore, give to them the possibility to participate to this project, would represent a great opportunity for them not only to acquire new tools and methodologies able to contrast these
phenomena, but also to compare their feeling and their situation/context with the ones of peers coming from different European countries.
Moreover, at the end of the project the Youth Department of the Municipality will organize a restitution event with the purpose to involve a bigger number of young people, informing
them about the activities of the project and the results obtained, having as principal characters of the event the youths who have partecipated to the mobility, in order to transfer the
tools acquired to the youths of Cento.

Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Asociación Patnia. Convivencia y Cultura Europeas was stablished in 2021 by a group of
young people with experience in Erasmus programs, pedagogy and inclusiveness. It is based
in the Southeast of Spain and is a non-profit organization, whose main objective is to
promote European values among the youth of the Mar Menor region.
The organization aims to achieve this goal through the following principles: encouraging
the participation of young people in European social, cultural and political life; bringing the
culture and traditions of European countries closer to Spanish citizens and sharing our
culture with communities from other countries. Additionally, Patnia has set a cross-cutting
objective in all its activities, which is the promotion and defense of the following values: equality, solidarity, multiculturalism,
volunteering, environmental conservation, human rights, diversity and social inclusion.

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
We're driven to undertake this project because of the pressing need to address the profound impact of media on the
attitudes, behaviors, and well-being of young people. The issues we aim to tackle revolve around the unchecked influence of
various media forms, from social platforms to traditional outlets, which often shape perceptions, influence decision-making,
and impact mental health.
Our project's objectives are multifaceted. Firstly, we aim to raise awareness about the complexities of media influence on
youth, highlighting both its positive and negative aspects. By doing so, we hope to empower young individuals with critical
thinking skills, enabling them to discern reliable sources, identify biases, and make informed choices about the media
content they engage with.
Moreover, our focus extends to improve media literacy among youth. We aim to equip them with the tools necessary to
navigate the digital landscape responsibly, emphasizing the potential of media as a catalyst for positive personal
development and societal change.
Another critical aspect of our project is the promotion of positive media campaigns that address social issues, promote
inclusivity, and encourage young individuals to actively participate in shaping social media narratives for the betterment of
themselves and their communities. Additionally, we seek to delve into the relationship between media exposure and mental
health, aiming to develop resources and initiatives that assist young people in managing the potential negative impact of
media on their well-being.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
Regarding the general objectives of the Erasmus + program, our initiative promotes intercultural communication and
education, a cornerstone of the Erasmus programme. By fostering international collaboration and engaging participants in
discussions about social media, we actively encourage the exchange of diverse ideas and cultures, as well as cultural
understanding and intercultural dialogue, which are core values of Erasmus+.
Our project aligns perfectly with the goals of youth empowerment advocated by the Erasmus Youth Exchanges. Through
providing non-formal education based activities, we aim to empower them, facilitating their personal and professional
growth.
The project promotes key competences, namely, critical thinking, media literacy and digital skills. These competences helps
the mentioned personal and professional growth.
One of the goals of our project is to empower young people to become active, informed, and responsible citizens in an
increasingly digital world, which directly supports Erasmus+ objectives related to fostering active citizenship, social
inclusion, and solidarity. By addressing key topics such as media literacy and the mental health impacts of media exposure,
our project also contributes to social change, empowering young participants to create positive media content and engage
in social campaigns that benefit their communities, further aligning with the Youth Exchanges' objectives of social cohesion
and active participation.
Impact
How will your project benefit the young participants involved in the project, during and after the project lifetime?
During the project: Participants will develop robust media literacy skills, enabling them to navigate media landscapes with a
critical eye, distinguishing between reliable and unreliable sources. They will feel empowered through active engagement in
discussions and activities that encourage positive media shaping. The workshops and activities will equip them with
practical skills in media creation, online safety, and digital citizenship. There will be many opportunity during the sessions to
enhance soft skills for the participants, among others, presentation and public speaking skills, teamwork skills, digital skills
and many more.
Furthermore, interacting with peers from diverse backgrounds will promote cultural exchange, understanding and empathy.
As well as, they can build international friendships and discover new cultures through participating the project.

Please describe the background of the participants in each participating group and how each group was formed. Please
also provide information on the group leaders, the age of the participants and how country balance is ensured. If
necessary, explain how the gender balance is respected.
A total of 35 young people from 5 countries will participate in the project, divided into teams of 6 participants and 1 group
leader each. The participants have a diverse socieconomic, linguistic and cultural backgrounds, which contributes to the
success of the project.
As this is the association's first project, we wanted fewer teams but more participants in the project. This makes
coordination easier for us on one hand, and on the other hand, ensures that we do not fall into the mistake of having less
than 4 participants per team. The age of the participants will be between 18 and 25 years old, and the project has been
planned based on the needs of this age group.
The requirement for group leaders is that they must be at least 18 years old, with no upper age limit apart from this. We will
hold a preliminary online meeting with them before the project to provide them with the necessary information. We are
looking for group leaders who already have experience in youth work and/or with Erasmus+ projects, as they will have a
crucial role in the reflection sessions and in the information flow.
The creation of teams will be the responsibility of the sending organizations. We have tried to select partners who work
directly with young people or have active members so that the selection can be made smoothly and genuinely motivated
young people can come to the project. It is very important that the selection is based on the motivation of the participants
and their interest in the topic, and we draw the attention of sending organizations to this. There will be an open call by the
partner organizations for the participant selection that will provide a democratic process and can encourage young people to
apply from different backgrounds. The partners will use their experience and expertise in the selection process, as each of
them has prior experience as a sending organization. Country balance is also important to us, meaning there will be an
equal number of participants from each country, representing different regions of Europe.
We aim to include at least 2 participants with fewer opportunities in each team. We believe that young people with fewer
opportunities are at even greater risk during unconscious media use, which is why we want to involve them as much as
possible in the project.
We pay close attention to gender balance; we definitely want an equal number of male and female participants, and we also
draw our partners' attention to this during the selection of participants.

Planning: during the planning process, we conducted a needs assessment in our association on what topics our members
would like to be involved in the organisation and implementation of a project, and what learning topics would be needed.
These participants are willing to contribute in every part of the project and working on it. The project was written with the
inputs and ideas by the members of the association.
Preparations: in preparation, we will ask participants about their diet, what motivates them, what they expect from the
project and what they would like to learn from it. They will also need to prepare for their intercultural evening and buy their
own flight tickets. We will send them articles and videos in advance in order to familiarize with the topic, as well as they will
be encouraged to do research in the topic.
Participants will work closely with the partner organizations and organizers in order to prepare with the logisitical, especially
regarding travel planning. There will be also a task in the WhatsApp group to start teambuilding, where everyone are
expected to send a meme about themselves, as a connection point with social media.
Implementation:
All the activities based on the participation of the participants. They will attend workshops, guest lecture, intercultural nights,
team-building activities and many more, with their active contribution.
At the beginning of the project, participants will be asked about their fears, expectations and what they would like to learn.
These, and their motivation letters, will be used to move the programme in a direction that best suits their needs. At the end
of each day, through the team leaders, we will ask participants for their feedback on what they have learned, how the day
went and what they would like to change. We will build-in these changes for the next day activities.
An anonymous box will also be set up where participants can anonymously share any questions they have about the
organisation. At the end of each activity, we will also ask for your feedback on the session.
Follow up: at the end of the project, participants will fill in a larger questionnaire at the last session about how the project
was like for them, what they learned, what they would change, how much they have improved. The YouthPass will be filled
in by each participant to make them aware of their own learning curve and learning process. And after the project, they will
be involved in the dissemination, which we will make mandatory. With this, they will helping to reach other young people
regarding the project and its results.
With the help of the alumni groups, participants will stay in touch with each other and with the organizers, which will help
maintain relationships and the skills they have developed.
What will the participants learn about the chosen topic of the activity? Which learning outcomes or competences (i.e.
knowledge, skills and attitudes/behaviours) are to be acquired/improved by participants in the activity?
The participants will learn about the complexities of media influence on youth, including both its positive and negative
aspects. They will acquire critical thinking skills to discern reliable media sources, identify biases, and make informed
choices about the content they engage with. Additionally, they will develop strong media literacy skills, enabling them to
navigate the digital landscape responsibly and use media as a tool for positive personal and societal development.
Participants will also gain practical skills in media creation, online safety, and digital citizenship. They will improve soft skills
such as presentation, public speaking, teamwork, and digital competencies. The multicultural and non-formal learning
environment will push them to step out of their comfort zones, fostering cultural understanding and empathy through
teamwork in international groups.
After the project, participants will retain and apply these skills in their daily lives, becoming more critical and responsible
media consumers, and actively creating positive media content. These skills will contribute to their personal and
professional development, as well as their ability to manage the potential negative impacts of media on their mental well-
being.
Regarding the key competences:
Multilingual Competence: During the youth exchange, the working language is English, which is not the native language of
any participating country. This in itself greatly enhances their foreign language speaking skills, especially in various
situations during the project, such as presentations of individual countries and debates, which also require quick thinking
and thus further develop their foreign language speaking skills. We made sure that there are no two countries with the same
language in the youth exchange, so everyone will use English to communicate with each other.
Digital Competence: Participants will need to use Kahoot, Google Drive, Google Form, email, and social media, among
others, which will help develop their digital competencies.
Personal, Social, and Learning Competence: Participants will encounter various non-formal methods from debates to
simulations, which will help them solve new situations and step out of their comfort zones, thereby learning new things
through unconventional methods.

Citizenship Competence: We encourage participants to create their own initiatives, projects, and take leadership roles in
their own communities, mainly through the dissemination but also beyond that.
Cultural Awareness and Expression: The entire duration of the project is filled with opportunities for cultural awareness and
expression, especially during the intercultural evenings , where representatives of each participating country can learn more
about other participant countries, their cultures, and cuisines. National drinks, foods, songs, and dances enrich these
evenings. As well as, there will be a lot of international teamwork during the sessions to help cultural awareness.
What are the basic elements of the activity? Please describe at the very least the venue(s), non formal learning methods
used, aims of the session etc.
The location of the project will be the Garden Panzió in Balatonkeresztúr. We have already personally inspected the
guesthouse during participation in a previous project, and we believe it is an excellent venue for hosting this project. The
guesthouse is close to the town, yet situated in nature. This is necessary for the project, as it provides a kind of digital detox
away from the noise and constant stimulation of the city. Our explicit aim with the project was not to be distracted by a larger
city. Yet, there is a shop, a pharmacy, a doctor and all other services that the participants might need nearby.
Only the participants of the project will be staying there, so we won’t disturb other guests with the project. The
accommodation is also near Lake Balaton, and it is no secret that one of our goals in choosing this location is to offer
participants a true summer experience and to show them the Hungarian sea. The swimming pool at the accommodation can
further enhance this summer experience. The guesthouse has rooms for 3, 4, and 5 people, which will be allocated by
gender but mixed by nationality. It is a great convenience for us that the accommodation staff will handle the meals, allowing
the project organizers to focus solely on facilitation, preparation, project management, administration, and other essential
elements of the project. The guesthouse has large spaces that facilitate the implementation of activities. The venue is
environmentally friendly, as selective waste collection is practiced, a no food waste policy is in place, and it is easily
accessible by train from Budapest.
Some of the most important workshops through the project:
Digital Vision Board:Participants create digital vision boards representing their ideal online relationships, encouraging
creativity and personal expression.
Digital Self-Care Toolkit: This practical workshop equips participants with tools and strategies to protect themselves online,
emphasizing the importance of self-care in the digital realm.
Unraveling the Threads: Exploring the Impact of Violent Media on Youth Behavior is an insightful session designed to delve
into the intricate relationships between exposure to violent media content and the behavior of young individuals. Participants
engage in discussions, case studies, and activities to comprehensively explore the psychological, social, and cultural
dimensions of this impact.
Fake News Detective:Participants create a digital vision board focusing on combatting misinformation. Through this
interactive session, they enhance digital literacy skills, emphasizing the importance of verifying information before sharing it
online.
Media Literacy Game: "Fact or Fiction?":This seminar introduces a captivating "Fact or Fiction?" game, fostering media
literacy by challenging participants to distinguish between factual and fictional information. Through interactive gameplay,
participants enhance their critical thinking skills and learn to navigate the vast digital information landscape.
SafeClick Seminar:Embark on a journey of digital safety with the SafeClick Seminar. This immersive session delves into the
intricacies of secure online practices, empowering participants with tools and strategies to navigate the digital realm safely.
Through a seminar format, interactive discussions, and a Q&A session, participants gain practical insights into fostering a
secure online presence.
Game: Cyber Sleuth Challenge::Elevate cyber awareness through the Cyber Sleuth Challenge, an interactive and
competitive game. Participants test their ability to identify and respond to cyber threats in a simulated online environment.
This game-based learning approach enhances digital competence, analytical skills, teamwork, and decision-making.
Media Influence Trivia:Ignite awareness with the Media Influence Trivia, an interactive session exploring the nuanced
impact of media on various aspects of life. This engaging trivia fosters critical thinking, analytical skills, and collaborative
learning as participants test their knowledge and broaden their understanding of media influence.

utilized not only before but also during and after the project by the participants. To promote efficiency, we will encourage
sending organizations and young people to form small national chat groups, Messenger or Whatsapp groups, and get in
touch with each other before the project starts, so they don't meet completely as strangers during the project. Additionally,
the project will assist them in their organizational tasks, such as preparing for the cultural evening, organizing travel, and
dissemination.
WhatsApp group: We will create 2 WhatsApp groups, one with the team leaders and representatives of the partner

teams, where team members can get to know each other, outline their personal expectations regarding the project, discuss

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
In every country, the groups are already acquainted with one another when they start the project. In this process, we would
like to request the support of our partners. They will get together and establish their own social media networks via group
chats on Facebook or Whatsapp before the project. The participants will receive an explanation of the project's objectives,
their role in it, and how they can benefit from it best. This information will be shared by partners, group leaders, the
Facebook group, and the opening online meeting. An online meeting will be held with each participant before the project by
the partner organisations to discuss with the participants the most important information about the project and what the
participants should prepare, what to bring, for example for the intercultural evening, as well as their travel arrangements and
any questions the participants may have.
Every individual will receive the infopack, containing information about the activities, targets, aims, and the country of
hosting, along with specifics regarding costs, transportation, insurance, hotel, and security. This will include all the pertinent
information about the project, including what to bring (e.g., games, food for the national evenings, towels, sunscreen, etc.).
There will also be some suggested routes from Budapest to the venue included in the infopack.It will contain information
about Hungary, including basic Hungarian phrases, as well as information about the venue, accomodation and food. The
Facebook and Whatsapp contacts of the organizers, as well as email addresses will be also shown in the Infopack, enabling
us to help them immediately in the event that they become lost while traveling.

that are very similar with the End-of-Project Questionnaire. We will ask the same questions as in that, but instead of a
Google Form, participants has to form groups for every question, based on their knowledge and understanding about social
media usage, on a scale 1-5.
We will take notes of this activity (as well as, pictures) to know the situation of social media awareness of the participants.
As mentioned, we will do this in a very relaxed way.
End-of-Project Questionnaire: At the project's end, participants receive a comprehensive questionnaire evaluating their
learning outcomes and the project's impact on social media influence. Questions include:
- Has your understanding of social media's impact on youth behaviors and mental health improved?
- Have your attitudes and behaviors regarding responsible social media use and online well-being changed?
- Have you acquired skills to manage social media's influence positively?
- How do you plan to apply knowledge gained for better social media use?
- Do you feel more empowered to navigate challenges posed by social media on youth well-being?
These methods assess personal development, attitude changes, increased awareness, and skill acquisition related to social
media influence.
To measure how these changed before and after the project, we will compare the results of the before- and after
questionnaires. As there will be not only subjective, but objective questions in both questionnaire, we can compare the
answers. We aim a 30% improvement in the answers, as one of our evaluation goal.
The goal is not just to measure success but to empower participants in making informed choices within the realm of social
media. We will consider the project a success if we have met the participants' expectations, their fears, expectations,
challenges from the beginning of the project, i.e. we have met the participants' expectations, they have overcome their fears
and they have successfully taken on the challenges. It is important for them to be aware of this, and we will give them all the
help we can.
In addition, we will hold a meeting with the group leaders every evening, where we will rely on their input as experienced
participants. After the project, also with them and the partner organisations, we will hold a final online meeting to discuss,
among other things, the success of the project and its results. An internal meeting will happen as well within the organization
and within those, who helped in the realization of the project.

Sustainability of the results
What will you do to make sure that your project continues to have effects also after it ends?
Firstly, we'll develop, together with the participants during the project, educational resources and materials to later use and
distribute during dissemination, future projects (especially the partner NGOs of the project) and in the participants
community, empowering them to advocate for responsible social media usage.
Additionally, we'll establish a strong social media presence to encourage ongoing engagement, information sharing, and
provide updates on social media trends and effects not just the participants but also other young people. An article on the
website of Street of Youth Association will be published and will be available for everyone for a longer time.
Moreover, we'll encourage participants to arrange local events and workshops in their communities, leveraging their
knowledge and skills to promote mindful and positive social media behaviors. With the active involvment of participants (and
partner organizations) to dissemination of the project, the project can have effects in the communities of the young people.
The communication channels of the project, namely, the WhatsApp and Facebook groups will be maintaned after the project
too, and will serve as alumni groups. This will enable communication between participants after the project too. Setting up
an alumni network, based on the social media groups, will enable former participants to maintain connections, exchange
insights, and collaborate on future initiatives related to social media influence.
Finally, building upon our experiences and lessons learned, we aim to create a larger and longer-lasting project with our
current partners, which might be a seconddition of the youth exchange, a training course for youth workers on the topic or a
KA210 project. This will be discussed after the evaluation of the project.
Are you planning measures to make sure that the results produced are used and beneficial to others beyond the project’s
lifetime? If yes, which ones?
We fully believe that the impact of our project will continue far after its planned completion date. To accomplish this, we have
thoughtfully developed several tangible steps that will spread the results of our efforts and guarantee their ongoing,
beneficial influence. The participants (through Google Drive) and the public (through Salto) will have easy access to the
instructional materials, toolkits, and tools developed as essential parts of our project. We would like to upload these
materials to the SALTO platform as well, in order to help other youth workers with projects related to social media usage. By
making these helpful resources freely available, we hope to support other NGOs with their projects and daily work.
As for the more tangible soft skills, it will be measured with online forms, as mentioned before. The rather intangible ones,
such as attitude, views will be measured through the reflection sessions and feedback sessions, as well as through open
conversations.
On the short term, learning skills will be measured through self-evaluation, after each acitivity, as well as at the end of the
days. For the after-the-activity evaluation, facilitators will give guidance, for the one at the end of the day, the team leaders.
On the longer term, feedback from partner organizations and data from them will be crucial, both regarding the organization
and regarding the participants they work with as youth workers. By data, we mean the events and workshop being
organized in the topic by them, also international projects, as well as new colalboration.
Beyond the material resources, we also actively support our project participants' sense of advocacy. Rather than confining
their involvement to the project's duration, we are motivating them to evolve into advocates for conscious media usage. One
way that this encouragement is put into practice is by motivating participants to plan seminars or local awareness
campaigns that concentrate on how the media shapes the opinions of young people. By instigating such grassroots
initiatives, we envision expanding the project's sphere of influence to a wider audience and promoting sustained awareness,
especially through the social media usage of them in the future. Importantly, we will also encourage participants to take the
initiative themselves and organise projects in their own environment. We hope that the multiplier effect will work and that the
project will inspire them to become active citizens themselves.
Essentially, our main objective is to ensure that the effects of our project last longer than the project duration and serve as
an ongoing source of encouragement for positive change. We hope to spread the values of inclusiveness and responsible
media influence on youth across a range of cultures and demographics with these carefully designed tools and practical
demonstrations.

Dissemination of project results
How will you make your project visible outside your organisation and partner organisations? How will you share its results
and success? With whom will you share the results?
We created a dissemination strategy with both online and offline elements to maximize the effect of the dissemination.
Throughout the project, we will share pictures and videos of the project every day, so that our youth exchange can reach
people who cannot be there in person but are interested. As well as, we and the partner organizations will share the
opportunity on their social media and websites, as well as the results of the project. We encourage participants to be active
in the sharing, not only after the project, but during the project too.
The specific communication channels will be the Instagram, Facebook, and TikTok accounts of our association and the
partner organizations, based on what we regularly use and what is currently most commonly used by young people.
Primarily, we will create short videos with musical backgrounds, mainly to engage young people. The main communication
focus will be on conscious media use and the relationship between young people and mental health. Additionally, we will
showcase the atmosphere of the Erasmus+ project and provide information about what an Erasmus+ youth exchange is
like, in order to introduce the program to as many young people as possible.
As for the visibility assessment, we will use reach indicators in order to assess if we reached our visibility goals. We aim to
reach 400 people in terms of views during the project through social media posts and stories, which will be shared by the
association, the partners, and the organizers. Overall, following the entire lifecycle of the project, we aim to reach 1,800
people. The number of views and interactions can be tracked using social media analytics. When defining the indicators, we
considered our own and our partner organizations' follower bases, the average view counts on our current posts, and a
mutual agreement among the organizers on what we expect of ourselves and how many people we want to reach with the
project’s message at a minimum. It is important to note that this is not a goal but the minimum expectation we set for
ourselves.
They will have 3 options to choose from, in order to reach as many people as possible with the results and lessons of the
project and the Erasmus+ programme itself:
- a text-based social media post describing their personal development and what they have learned and experienced
- a Youtube or TikTok video with similar aims
- an offline dissemination event, held in their own environment, with the participation of at least 5 young people, related to
the project theme. The aim is to enable participants to pass on some of the lessons learned to their peers.
Since it is a project about media, we take very seriously the visibility. As it was previously mentioned in another question, we
will use specific hashtags, will make high-quality photos and will create many materials that can be used later for increasing
visibility. For this reason, we will also share an article on our website about the project, alongside with pictures, as well as
encourage the project partners for this too.
How will you involve participants in such activities?
By sharing their experiences both offline and online, participants contribute significantly to the word-of-mouth campaign. On
social media, we ask them to write, share videos and images related to their experiences and lessons learned. We also
dedicate some time during the week to brainstorm with each other and plan their posts, stories and short videos, in order to
foster creativity and help each other, as we believe it’s a more effective way than doing this only individually and better ideas
can be created for the dissemination.
It's also advised that they discuss it with their friends and family. We will also create a Google Drive, where everyone can
upload their photos and videos, therefore, content creation will be easier to every participant, even for those, who are not the
best in photographing or doesn’t have a good quality camera.
We are making it mandatory for participants to share what they have learnt in order to increase the impact of this sharing
with their dissemination. They have 3 options, as mentioned before.
With our free day in Keszthely and with our city quest, we aim to gain visibility among those who haven’t heard about
Erasmus+ or about youth exchange programs, so the participants of the project can engage with them naturally.
We view participants as agents of good change in addition to being learners. They encourage people to develop and learn
by sharing their own experiences. It's crucial to use this ripple effect to reach more people. Our goal is to reach a wider
audience and leave a greater impression by means of these shared stories.
To put it simply, everyone is expected to contribute in order to share and learn.

Erasmus+ Youth Quality Standards
Organisations implementing Erasmus learning mobility activities must adhere to a set of quality standards. These standards
exist to ensure quality experience and learning outcomes for Erasmus participants, and to make sure that all organisations
receiving the Programme’s funding are contributing to its objectives.
I. POLICY PRIORITIES IN THE FIELD OF YOUTH :
Beneficiary organisations should contribute to the overall objectives of the EU Youth Strategy [1] and to the European Youth
Goals developed within this frame. They should apply the guiding principles laid down in the EU Youth Strategy.
II. BASIC PRINCIPLES OF THE ERASMUS PROGRAMME :
• Inclusion and diversity: beneficiary organisations must respect the principle of inclusion and diversity in all aspects
of their activities. Whenever possible, beneficiary organisations should open their activities to participants from
different backgrounds and with diverse abilities, actively engage and involve participants with fewer opportunities.
• Environmental sustainability and responsibility: beneficiary organisations must make efforts to design activities
in an environmentally friendly way and promote environmentally sustainable and responsible behaviour among its
participants.
• Virtual cooperation, virtual mobility and blended mobility: beneficiary organisations should integrate the use of
digital tools and learning methods to complement the physical mobility activities or to improve the cooperation with
partner organisations.
• Active participation in the network of Erasmus organisations: beneficiary organisations should seek to
become active members of the Erasmus network by taking part in activities organised by the National Agency or
other organisations. Experienced Erasmus beneficiaries should share their knowledge with organisations who have
no or little experience in the programme, providing them with advice, mentorship or other support. Beneficiary
organisations should also encourage their participants to take part in Erasmus community building activities.
III. QUALITY MANAGEMENT
• Responsibility: beneficiary organisations will be responsible for results and quality of the implemented activities,
regardless of the involvement of any other organisations or individuals. During the implementation of Erasmus
activities, beneficiary organisations must take responsibility for key decisions on all tasks that directly affect the
outcomes of the implemented activities, especially in relation to these quality standards. The core tasks include
financial management of the programme funds, contact with the National Agency, reporting on implemented
activities, as well as all decisions that directly affect the content, quality and results of the implemented activities.
• Transparency: beneficiary organisations may receive advice, assistance or services from other organisations or
individuals, as long as it keeps control of the content, quality and results of the implemented activities. If the
beneficiary organisations are using the programme funds to pay for such services, rights and obligations of both
parties (in particular tasks to be carried out, quality control mechanisms and consequences in case of poor or failed
delivery on part of the service provider) must be formally defined and available for review by the National Agency.
• Partnerships: While overall responsibility of the implementation lies within the beneficiary organisations, activities
should be planned, implemented and evaluated together with all partner organisations involved. Clear definition of
roles, and distribution of tasks should be set-up and agreed.
• Contributions paid by participants: as a form of co-funding, the beneficiary organisation may ask participants in
activities for contributions to pay for goods and services necessary for the implementation of those activities. The size
of the participants’ contributions must remain proportional to the grant awarded for the implementation of the activity,
must be clearly justified, collected on a non-profit basis, and may not create unfair barriers to participation (especially
concerning participants with fewer opportunities). Additional fees or other participant contributions cannot be
collected by other service providers chosen by the beneficiary organisation.
• Monitoring and evaluation: beneficiary organisations should put in place adequate plans and procedures to
monitor and evaluate the activities, to enable them to assess how they are progressing towards their objectives and
whether they have reached them. The monitoring and evaluation outcomes should lead to improvements in the
implementation of ongoing or future activities.
• Building capacity and integrating results of the activities in the organisation: beneficiary organisations
should take steps to use the results of the implemented activities in their regular activities, to build their capacity and
the capacity of their staff.

• Gathering and using participants’ feedback: beneficiary organisations should encourage the participants to fulfil
the standardised participant reports provided by the European Commission, and should make use of the feedback
provided by the participants to improve future activities.
IV. QUALITY LEARNING ACTIVITIES
This section sets the minimum standards to follow. Further guidance and tools are available to support the delivery of high
quality learning mobility activities, such as the Quality Framework for Learning Mobility.
• Practical arrangements: beneficiary organisations must ensure quality of practical and logistic arrangements
(travel, accommodation, insurance, safety, visa applications, social security, etc.). If these tasks are delegated to the
participants or another participating organisation, beneficiary organisations will remain ultimately responsible for
verifying their provision and quality.
• Health, safety and respect of applicable regulation: all activities must be organised with a high standard of
safety and protection for involved participants and must respect all applicable regulation (for example regarding
parental consent etc.). The beneficiary organisations must ensure that their participants have appropriate insurance
coverage, as defined by the general rules of the Programme and the applicable regulation.
• Selection of participants: participants must be selected through a transparent, fair and inclusive selection
procedure.
• Preparation and support of participants: participants must receive appropriate preparation in terms of practical,
cultural and safety aspects. They should be fully aware of what is expected from them before, during and after the
activity. Adequate guidance and support must be available during all phases of the activities.
• Support to participants with fewer opportunities: beneficiary organisations must ensure fair and equal
conditions for all participants, including those facing barriers (such as special learning needs or physical disability).
• Participatory approach: participants should - as far as possible - be actively involved in all phases of the activities
and receive adequate support to do so.
• Participatory methods: activities should be based on participatory methods and offer space for interaction of
participants, sharing of ideas, avoiding passive listening and allow them to contribute to the activities with their own
knowledge and skills, reversing the traditional roles of outside "experts".
• Linguistic support: beneficiary organisations must ensure appropriate language training, adapted to the needs of
the participants. Where appropriate, the beneficiary organisation should make maximum use of the specific tools and
funding provided by the Programme for this purpose.
• Definition and monitoring of learning outcomes: the expected learning outcomes must be clearly defined for
each participant or group of participants prior to the activity. Where relevant, participants should be involved in the
identification of the expected learning outcomes. Progress in the learning process should be monitored and adequate
support should be provided to participants in this regard.
• Identification and recognition of learning outcomes: non-formal and informal learning outcomes gained by
participants in the activities should be identified and documented, in particular through EU level recognition tools
such as Youthpass. Youthpass is a tool to support validation of non-formal and informal learning outcomes. It is a
part of the European Commission's strategy to foster the recognition of non-formal learning. Youthpass
V. Sharing results and knowledge about the programme
• Sharing results within the organisation: beneficiary organisations should make their participation in the Erasmus
programme widely known within the organisation and create opportunities for participants to share their mobility
experience with their peers.
• Sharing results with other organisations and the public: beneficiary organisations should share the results of
their activities with other organisations and the public.
• Publicly acknowledging European Union funding: beneficiary organisations should make their participation in
the Erasmus programme known in their community and in the wider public. Beneficiary organisations must also
inform all participants about the source of the fund.
